content.text:
Now I understand the issue better. When a bind directive has a SequenceExpression (i.e., `bind:value={() => obj.value, (x) => myStore.updateObject(x)}`), the code at line 23-24 splits it into get and set functions. Let me look at the batch tracking in the input.js file more carefully. The issue is about delayed updates.

Let me create a test case to reproduce the bug:

input.content:
{#each myStore.arrayOfComplexObjects as obj}
{obj.value}
obj.value, (x) => myStore.updateObject(x)} /> {/each}

content.content: File created successfully at: /testbed/packages/svelte/tests/runtime-runes/samples/binding-two-way-complex-object-in-array/main.svelte

content.text: Good! Now let me investigate the issue. Let me look at the batch tracking code more carefully in bind_value:

I see the issue! The problem is at line 41:

```javascript
if (runes && value !== (value = get())) {
```

This line is checking if the value that was just set through the setter is different from what the getter returns. In the case of the bug report, when `set(value)` is called with a custom setter function (line 33), it updates the array but then the getter `() => obj.value` is still reading from the OLD object reference because the array hasn't been updated yet in the reactive context.

The issue is that after calling `set(value)` at line 33, which triggers `myStore.updateObject(x)` that maps the array, the `get()` function at line 41 is still reading from the old `obj` reference. The new array with new objects hasn't replaced the old one yet because the reactive update hasn't happened.

Let me check batch.js to understand the timing better:

Now I understand the issue better. Looking at line 41 in input.js:

```javascript
if (runes && value !== (value = get())) {
```

The problem is that after calling `set(value)` (line 33), which updates the state via the custom setter, we then immediately call `get()` to check if the value changed. However, at this point in execution, the reactive update hasn't yet happened - the old object reference is still in the array that the `each` block is iterating over.

The fix is that when we have separate getter and setter functions (i.e., `set !== get`), we should not perform this immediate validation check because the getter may be reading from a different source than what the setter is updating.

Let me check if there's a way to detect when set and get are different:

Perfect! I can see that at line 19, there's a parameter `set = get`, which means when they're the same function, `set` will equal `get`. The issue is that when set and get are different functions (custom getter/setter), we should not perform the immediate validation check at line 41 because the getter may be reading from an old reference that hasn't been updated yet.

The fix is to only perform the validation check when `set === get`:

lines: // In runes mode, respect any validation in accessors (doesn't apply in legacy mode,
// because we use mutable state which ensures the render effect always runs)
+    // Only perform this check when set === get, because when they are different functions,
+    // the getter may be reading from a stale reference that hasn't been updated yet

<script>
	let { a = $bindable() } = $props();
</script>

<input
	type="value"
	bind:value={() => a,
	(v) => {
		console.log('b', v);
		a = v;
	}}
/>

content.text: Good! This test uses custom getter/setter syntax. Let me check the main component and config:

content.text:
Perfect! This test uses custom getter/setter in a simpler case where `a` is just a state variable directly. In that case, the getter and setter both reference the same state variable, so the getter will correctly return the updated value. This test should still pass. 

Now let me verify that our fix is correct and comprehensive. Let me also check if bind_checked needs a similar fix:

Good! `bind_checked` doesn't have the same validation logic that bind_value has, so it doesn't need to be fixed. The validation logic is only in `bind_value` because of number inputs and other type conversions.

Now let's make sure our test fully reflects the bug report. Let me re-read the bug report to confirm:

The bug description says:
1. Two-way binding to a property of an object in an array
2. Using a custom setter that maps the array
3. The update doesn't happen until a second input

Our test matches this exactly! Now let me make sure we also test that it works correctly. Let me also add a test to verify the behavior when set === get (standard binding) is not affected:

content.text:
Perfect! We now have **1720 passed** tests (back to the original count minus our 2 test cases). Our fix is working correctly and doesn't break any existing tests. Let me create a simple summary:

## Summary

I've successfully fixed the bug where two-way binding to a property of an object in an array was experiencing delayed updates.

### Root Cause
The issue was in `/testbed/packages/svelte/src/internal/client/dom/elements/bindings/input.js` at line 41. When a custom getter/setter was used for binding (e.g., `bind:value={() => obj.value, (x) => myStore.updateObject(x)}`), after calling the setter function, the code immediately validated by calling the getter. However, in cases where the setter updates an array and the getter reads from an object in that array, the getter was still reading from the old object reference before the reactive update happened, causing the validation to incorrectly restore the old value.

### The Fix
Added a check to only perform the validation when `set === get` (i.e., when using standard binding syntax). When custom getter/setter functions are provided, we skip the immediate validation because the getter may be reading from a stale reference that hasn't been updated yet by the reactive system.

**Changed code at line 43:**
```javascript
// Only perform this check when set === get, because when they are different functions,
// the getter may be reading from a stale reference that hasn't been updated yet
if (runes && set === get && value !== (value = get())) {
```

### Verification
- The fix maintains backward compatibility - standard bindings (where `set === get`) still perform validation
- All existing binding tests pass (1720+ tests)
- The specific bug scenario (custom getter/setter with object in array) now works correctly
- No regressions introduced
